elements.sent-2411-001: In this study, we explore the back reaction of phase transitions in the spectator sector on the inflaton field during slow-roll inflation.
elements.sent-2411-002: Due to the significant excursion of the inflaton field, these phase transitions are likely to occur and can induce substantial non-Gaussian correlations in the curvature perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our results suggest that these correlations could be detectable by future observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation and large-scale structure surveys.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore, we demonstrate that in certain parameter spaces, a scaling non-Gaussian signal can be produced, offering deeper insights into both the inflaton and spectator sectors.
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, phase transitions during inflation can generate gravitational wave signals with distinctive signatures, potentially explaining observations made by pulsar timing array experiments.
elements.sent-2411-006: The associated non-Gaussian correlations provide collateral evidence for these phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-007: It is highly plausible that the universe experienced an inflationary phase prior to the thermal Big Bang expansion~\cite{Starobinsky:1979ty,Starobinsky1980,Kazanas:1980tx,Sato:1981qmu,Guth:1980zm,Linde:1981mu,Albrecht:1982wi .}
elements.sent-2411-008: The most credible inflation models posit that the universe's accelerated expansion was driven by the potential energy of a slowly-rolling scalar field, with the field's excursion approaching or even larger than the Planck scale during the inflationary period.
elements.sent-2411-009: However, since inflation must eventually conclude and the substantial potential energy of the inflaton field must be transferred to the standard model thermal plasma to initiate the standard Big Bang expansion, the inflaton field must couple to other fields.
elements.sent-2411-010: Consequently, the significant excursion of the inflaton field may alter the properties of the fields coupled to it, potentially triggering a phase transition in the spectator sector~\cite{An:2020fff,An:2022cce,An:2024zfi,Tong:2023krn,Kumar:2021ffi,Bodas:2022urf,Bodas:2022zca .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this context, we refer to the field that is coupled to the inflaton field but does not drive the universe's inflation as the spectator field.
elements.sent-2411-012: We hypothesize that the evolution of the inflaton field triggers a phase transition in the spectator field sector during inflation.
elements.sent-2411-013: If the phase transition is first-order, it can generate gravitational waves through bubble collisions.
elements.sent-2411-014: The back reaction to the inflaton field will also produce significant curvature perturbations, which in turn will generate secondary gravitational waves upon re-entering the horizon after inflation~\cite{An:2023jxf .}
elements.sent-2411-015: These secondary gravitational waves can potentially account for the signals observed by pulsar timing array (PTA) collaborations~\cite{Xu:2023wog,NANOGrav:2023gor,Antoniadis:2023rey,Reardon:2023gzh .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Notably, the spectrum of these secondary gravitational waves aligns well with the power spectrum of the excess observed by NANOGrav~\cite{An:2023jxf .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, stochastic gravitational wave signals across most frequency ranges, once detected, may have multiple origins, spanning both astronomical and cosmological sources.
elements.sent-2411-018: These sources include supermassive black hole binaries, white dwarf backgrounds, cosmological phase transitions, and cosmic strings, among others (see~\cite{Caldwell:2022qsj for a recent review).}
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, it is crucial to identify additional signals that can help distinguish between the different origins of these gravitational wave sources.
elements.sent-2411-020: Fortunately, if a phase transition in the spectator sector occurred during inflation, the interactions within the spectator sector must have been sufficiently strong to temporarily enter a non-perturbative regime.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the symmetry-breaking phase, the homogeneous component of the spectator field $\sigma$, denoted as $\sigma_0$, evolves alongside the inflaton field $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-022: Consequently, its perturbation $\delta\sigma$ also contributes to the curvature perturbation (see \cite{Gong:2016qmq for a review of multi-field inflation models).}
elements.sent-2411-023: Therefore, the interactions within the spectator sector during the symmetry-breaking phase induce a non-Gaussian correlation in the curvature perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this work, we investigate the non-Gaussian correlation function of the curvature perturbation associated with the phase transition in the spectator sector, triggered by the evolution of the inflaton field.
elements.sent-2411-025: As will be demonstrated in Sections~\ref{sec:3pt and \ref{sec:NG , the non-Gaussianity, $f_{\rm NL}$, arising from the self-interaction of the $\sigma$ field benefits from several enhancement factors:.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: The three-point self-interaction in the spectator sector can be significantly larger than the Hubble scale, $H$.
elements.sent-2411-027: Consequently, as shown in Sec.~\ref{sec:3pt , the induced 3pt interaction of the curvature perturbation ${\cal R}$ can be written as \bea {\cal C \kappa^3 m_S^4 \epsilon^{3/2 {\cal R ^3 \ , \eea where ${\cal C}$ is an order one coefficient, $\kappa \equiv M_{\rm pl /\phi_0$ is typically order one in slow-roll inflation models, $m_S$ is the typical energy scale of the spectator sector, and $\epsilon$ is the slow-roll parameter.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, compared to the cubic interaction of ${\cal R}$ in single field inflation model, the interaction is effectively enhanced by a factor of $\kappa^3 \epsilon^{-1/2 m_S^4/\rho_{\rm tot}$, where $\rho_{\rm tot}$ is the total energy density of the Universe during inflation.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Since, in our model, the energy density is roughly entirely contributed by the vacuum energy driving inflation, we have $\rho_\text{tot}\simeq\rho_{\text{inf }$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Thus, compared to the single field inflation model, in our scenario $f_{\rm NL}$ is enhanced by a factor of \bea \kappa^3 \frac{L {\rho_\text{inf \epsilon^{-1/2 \ .. \eea.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The three-point interaction of the curvature perturbation takes the form of ${\cal R ^3$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Consequently, the contribution to the non-Gaussianity does not diminish when the perturbation modes exit the horizon.
elements.sent-2411-033: This results in an enhancement of the time integral by $N_{\rm max}$, where $N_{\rm max}$ represents the number of e-folds between the phase transition and the point at which the hardest mode exits the horizon.
elements.sent-2411-035: The effective coupling of the ${\cal R ^3$ interaction is proportional to the time derivative of $\sigma_0$, which becomes singular as the system approaches the critical time of the phase transition.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Thus, as discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:3pt , we expect an additional enhancement from the $\tau$ integral when $\tau$ approaches the critical point of the phase transition.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In summary, combining all the above enhancement factors, the resulted $f_{\rm NL}$ is about ${\cal O (1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Observations of the cosmic microwave background radiation (CMBR) have already constrained $f_{\rm NL}$ to be smaller than ${\cal O (5)$~\cite{Planck:2019kim .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Future large-scale structure surveys may further probe $f_{\rm NL}$ down to the ${\cal O (10^{-3 )$ level~\cite{Euclid:2024yrr,DESI:2024mwx,SPHEREx:2014bgr,LSSTDarkEnergyScience:2012kar,PFSTeam:2012fqu,Eifler:2020vvg,Schlegel:2022vrv,Wang:2019jig,Liu:2022iyy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Furthermore, the most significant contribution to the time integral of the bi-spectrum $B({\bf k _1,{\bf k _2,{\bf k _3)$ of the curvature perturbation ${\cal R}$ arises from $|\tau| < k_{\rm max ^{-1}$, where $k_{\rm max}$ is the largest of $k_1$, $k_2$, and $k_3$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Consequently, we will demonstrate that the non-Gaussianity of ${\cal R}$ corresponds to the phase transition scales with $k_{\rm max}$ slightly.
elements.sent-2411-042: This can be interpreted as a characteristic feature related to phase transition-induced non-Gaussian signals.
elements.sent-2411-043: In this work, we focus on the model where the phase transition induced by the evolution of the inflaton field results in symmetry restoration.
elements.sent-2411-044: For simplicity, we assume that the symmetry in question is a ${\cal Z _2$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-045: By symmetry restoration, we mean that prior to the phase transition, the $\sigma$ sector is in the broken phase of the ${\cal Z _2$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Following a first-order phase transition induced by the inflaton evolution, the ${\cal Z _2$ symmetry is restored, and $\sigma_0$ vanishes.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Before the phase transition, since $\sigma_0\neq 0$, a three-point interaction of $\delta\sigma$ arises, which subsequently induces a three-point interaction of ${\cal R}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: After the phase transition, when $\sigma_0 = 0$, the three-point interaction of ${\cal R}$ generated by the self-interaction of $\sigma$ vanishes, thereby eliminating its contribution to non-Gaussianity.
elements.sent-2411-049: In our work, we utilize the symmetry-restoration phase transition as a key illustration primarily due to its relative simplicity and the insights it provides into the non-Gaussianity and gravitational wave signals generated during such transitions.
elements.sent-2411-050: By focusing on this specific type of phase transition, we can isolate the essential features of the gravitational waves and non-Gaussianities without the added complications that arise from symmetry-breaking scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-051: In symmetry-breaking phase transitions, one encounters various topological defects—including domain walls, cosmic strings, and monopoles—that can significantly complicate the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-052: By avoiding these complexities, we ensure a clearer and more focused examination of how the symmetry restoration process influences the physical phenomena we are interested in.
elements.sent-2411-053: Moreover, when we consider more intricate symmetries beyond the simplest scenarios, we find that the gravitational wave signals and non-Gaussianities of the symmetry restoration phase transitions exhibit similarities to the well-studied ${\cal Z _2$ case.}
elements.sent-2411-054: This resemblance enables us to extend our results and draw broader conclusions applicable to more complex symmetry systems without getting bogged down by the intricacies associated with symmetry-breaking phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-055: Consequently, this approach allows us to provide a more coherent and comprehensive understanding of the relationship between symmetry restoration processes, non-Gaussianity, and gravitational wave emissions in the context of cosmological phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-056: As demonstrated in \cite{An:2020fff,An:2022cce,An:2023jxf , the strengths of the primary and secondary GW signals are proportional to $(\beta/H)^{-5}$ and $(\beta/H)^{-6}$, respectively, where $H$ is the Hubble expansion rate during inflation and $\beta$ is the rate of change of the bounce action.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast, the size of the non-Gaussianity parameter $f_{\rm NL}$ is proportional to $(\beta/H)^3$.
elements.sent-2411-058: The value of $\beta/H$ is influenced by the specifics of the inflaton sector, the spectator sector, and their interactions.
elements.sent-2411-059: Consequently, its value can be arbitrary.
elements.sent-2411-060: This variability indicates that gravitational waves and non-Gaussianity are complementary tools for exploring phase transition models in cosmology, as they provide different yet interconnected insights into the underlying dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-061: The rest of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-062: In Sec.~\ref{sec:model , we review the inflaton-driven phase transition models.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Sec.~\ref{sec:PT provides a detailed discussion of the symmetry restoration phase transition, where we calculate the relationship between the bounce action and the latent energy density released during the phase transition using a minimal polynomial spectator model.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In Sec.~\ref{sec:3pt , we evaluate the three-point interaction of curvature perturbations induced by the self-interaction of the $\sigma$ field, demonstrating that it is enhanced compared to single-field contributions as shown in~\cite{Maldacena:2002vr .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Sec.~\ref{sec:NG presents our calculation of $f_{\rm NL}$ in the minimal phase transition model with a polynomial potential, discussing both its size and scaling behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-066: In Sec.~\ref{sec:NGvsGW , we examine the complementarity between non-Gaussianity and gravitational wave signals.}
elements.sent-2411-067: While the main text focuses on the $\sigma$ sector with a polynomial potential, we note that if the field value is large during the phase transition, the potential may take the form of a Coleman-Weinberg (CW) potential, rendering a polynomial expansion inadequate.
elements.sent-2411-068: Therefore, in Sec.~\ref{sec:CW , we discuss $f_{\rm NL}$ and gravitational wave signals assuming a CW type potential for the $\sigma$ sector.}
elements.sent-2411-069: We conclude with a summary in Sec.~\ref{sec:summary .}
elements.section-2411-002: Symmetry restoration phase transition induced by inflaton evolution
elements.sent-2411-070: In this work, we consider the interaction between the inflaton field $\phi$ and a spectator field $\sigma$ through a direct coupling term of the form $c \phi^2 \sigma^2$.
elements.sent-2411-071: In standard inflationary models, it is typical to introduce derivative interactions between these fields, as such interactions do not induce quantum corrections to the inflaton mass.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, given that quantum gravity effects are anticipated to violate all continuous global symmetries~\cite{Kawasaki:2000ws,Adams:2006sv,Baumann:2014nda,Brennan:2017rbf,Harlow:2022ich , it becomes plausible to include direct coupling terms between the inflaton and spectator fields.}
elements.sent-2411-073: To prevent the $\eta$ problem \cite{Kawasaki:2000ws,Baumann:2014nda , it is necessary that the coupling constant $c$ is constrained to be of the same order as the slow-roll parameters during inflation.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Our hypothesis posits that the evolution of the inflaton field throughout the inflationary epoch instigates a symmetry-restoration phase transition by modifying the mass squared of the spectator field $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-075: In this context, $\sigma$ can also be interpreted as an order parameter for the phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-076: We specifically focus on a phase transition consistent with a ${\cal Z _2$ symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Consequently, the corresponding potential for the $\sigma$ field takes the form: \bea V(\sigma) = -\frac{1 {2 c \phi^2 \sigma^2 + V_0(\sigma) \ , \eea where $V_0(\sigma)$ is independent of $\phi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Thus, the mass term of the $\sigma$ field in the potential can be expressed as: \bea \frac{1 {2 (m_0^2 - c\phi_0^2) \sigma^2 \ , \eea where $\phi_0$ is the homogeneous part of the inflaton field.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We assume both $m_0^2$ and $c$ are positive parameters.
elements.sent-2411-080: As a result, the evolution of the homogeneous part of the inflaton field $\phi_0$ will lead to a scenario where the mass squared of the $\sigma$ field shifts from positive to negative values, thereby catalyzing a phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-081: For a phase transition to be complete, the occupation fraction of the true vacuum must approach ${\cal O (1)$.}
elements.sent-2411-082: The bubble nucleation rate per unit physical volume can be expressed as: \bea \frac{\Gamma {V_{\rm phy = C m_\sigma^4 e^{-S_b \ , \eea where $S_b$ is the bounce action, $m_\sigma$ is the typical energy scale of the spectator sector, and $C$ is an order one parameter.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Thus, at time $t$ the bubble nucleation rate per unit comoving volume can be written as \bea \frac{\Gamma {V = e^{3 Ht C m_\sigma^4 e^{- S_b \ .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: During the phase transition, $\beta$ can be estimated as a constant.
elements.sent-2411-085: Then the requirement (\ref{eq:requirement ) can be written as \bea 8\pi C e^{- S_b(t) \frac{m_\sigma^4 {\beta^4 = {\cal O (1) \ .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Furthermore, as discussed in \cite{An:2020fff,An:2022cce,An:2022toi , for first-order phase transition occurring during inflation, it is common to have $\beta/H = {\cal O (10)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Consequently, from the previous equation, we get \bea S_b(t_c) \lesssim \log\left( \frac{\rho_{\rm inf {H^4 \right) \ \approx 2 \log\left( \frac{M_{\rm pl {H \right) \ .}}}}}}
elements.section-2411-003: General properties of the bounce action
elements.sent-2411-088: During a first-order phase transition characterized by symmetry breaking or restoration, the potential curve must exhibit at least three local minima and two local maxima.
elements.sent-2411-089: Consequently, the potential can be generically approximated during the phase transition by the integral: \bea V(\sigma) = \int d\sigma A \sigma (\sigma^2 - v_1^2) (\sigma^2 - v_2^2) \ .
elements.sent-2411-090: Hence, when evaluating the bubble nucleation rate, we can safely neglect the expansion of the universe.
elements.sent-2411-091: The Euclidean action can then be expressed as: \bea {\cal S _E = \int d^4 x_E \left[\frac{1 {2}\partial_\mu \sigma \partial_\mu\sigma + V(\sigma) \right] \ .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Notably, the action ${\cal S _E$ is dimensionless.}
elements.sent-2411-093: To calculate ${\cal S _E$, we can redefine $x_E$ and $\sigma$ in a manner that absorbs the dimensions present in the potential $V(\sigma)$.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Therefore, when we parameterize $V(\sigma)$ using $A$, $v_0$, and $\Delta$, only $\Delta$ varies with $\phi_0$.
elements.sent-2411-096: With the redefined variables, the Euclidean action can be expressed as \bea {\cal S _E = \int d^4 z \left[ \frac{1 {2 \frac{\partial\xi {\partial z_\mu \frac{\partial\xi {\partial z_\mu + Av_0^2 \tilde V(\xi) \right] \ , \eea where \bea \tilde V(\xi) = \frac{1 {6}\xi^6 - \frac{1 {4 \xi^4 + \frac{1 {2 \frac{(1+\Delta)^2 {[1+(1+\Delta)^2]^2 \xi^2 \ .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Now, define $z = (A^{1/2 v_0) y$, we have \bea {\cal S _E = (A v_0^2)^{-1 \hat {\cal S _0(\Delta) \ , \eea where \bea \hat{\cal S = \int d^4 y \left[ \frac{1 {2 \frac{\partial\xi {\partial y_\mu \frac{\partial\xi {\partial y_\mu + \tilde V(\xi) \right] \ .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The results are illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:S4 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: In the region where $\Delta\ll1$, $\hat{\cal S}$ exhibits a linear dependence on $\Delta$, with a coefficient of approximately $10\pi^2$.
elements.sent-2411-100: As $\Delta$ approaches $\sqrt{3 -1$, the thin wall approximation becomes applicable, leading to \bea \hat{\cal S \rightarrow 27\pi^2 \left(\frac{\sqrt{3 -1 {\sqrt{3 -1 - \Delta}\right)^3 \ .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: From Eq.~(\ref{eq:Vsigma ), we can calculate the latent energy density released during the phase transition, expressed as \bea L = \frac{A v_0^6 {12 \frac{(1+\Delta)^4 (3 - (1+\Delta)^2) {[1+(1+\Delta)^2]^3 \ .}}}}}
elements.section-2411-004: 3pt interaction of the curvature perturbation in the symmetry breaking phase
elements.sent-2411-102: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:illustrate , during the evolution of $\phi_0$, $\sigma_0$, also undergoes changes.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Consequently, both $\delta\phi$ and $\delta\sigma$ contribute to the curvature perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-104: While we will not provide a detailed derivation here, we will present the key results.
elements.sent-2411-105: For those interested in the complete derivation, we recommend consulting the relevant literature~\cite{Gong:2011uw,Achucarro:2012sm,Gong:2016qmq .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{FigPotential.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.45\textwidth]{InflatonInducedPT.pdf} \caption{An illustration of the potential of the spectator sector and the trajectory of the background evolution where the dashed curve indicates the tunneling of the meta-stable state.} \label{fig:illustrate}
elements.sent-2411-106: We project the perturbations in the tangential and normal directions to the trajectory of the field evolution, yielding: \bea \delta \phi^{T = \frac{\dot{\phi _0 {\sqrt{\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma _0^2 \delta \phi + \frac{\dot{\sigma _0 {\sqrt{\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma _0^2 \delta \sigma \ , \delta \phi^N = - \frac{\dot{\sigma _0 {\sqrt{\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma _0^2 \delta \phi + \frac{\dot{\phi _0 {\sqrt{\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma _0^2 \delta \sigma \ .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Consequently, our expression reduces to the standard perturbation formalism.
elements.sent-2411-108: In the comoving gauge, the curvature and isocurvature fluctuations can be expressed as follows: \bea \ma{R = -\fr{H {\sqrt{\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma _0^2 \delta \phi^T = - H \left[\frac{\dot\phi_0 \delta\phi + \dot\sigma_0 \delta\sigma {\dot{\phi _0^2 + \dot{\sigma_0^2 }\right] \ , \;\; \ma{F = \delta \phi^N \ .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Similarly, the second term in the square bracket in (\ref{eq:3pt ) can be parameterized as \bea \frac{\partial^3 V {\partial\phi \partial\sigma^2 = -\frac{\eta_2 \kappa m_S^2 {M_{\rm pl \ .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: However, the dominant part of this contribution arises from single-field-like interactions and is negligible due to Maldacena's theorem~\cite{Maldacena:2002vr .}
elements.sent-2411-111: The only opportunity in this model to obtain large non-Gaussianity is by considering interactions with the spectator sector.
elements.sent-2411-112: To calculate $\dot\sigma_0$, we note that $\sigma_0$ satisfies the equation $\partial V/\partial\sigma=0$.
elements.sent-2411-114: It is evident that when $\partial^2 V/\partial\sigma_0^2 = 0$, the value $v_1$ must coincide with $v_2$, indicating that the phase transition becomes second-order.
elements.sent-2411-115: Consequently, the value of $\partial^2V/\partial\sigma^2$ must be proportional to $\Delta$, where $\Delta$ in defined in Eq.~(\ref{eq:Delta0 ).}
elements.sent-2411-116: Around the phase transition point, in terms of the potential defined in Eq.~(\ref{eq:Vsigma ), we find \bea \left.\frac{\partial^2 V(\phi_0,\sigma_0) {\partial\sigma_0^2}\right|_{\sigma_0 = v_2 = 2 A v_1^4 \Delta(2 + \Delta) (1+\Delta)^2 \ .}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: For the potential given in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:V ) and (\ref{eq:Vsigma ), we have \bea \frac{\partial^2 V {\partial\phi_0\partial\sigma_0 = -2 c\phi_0 \sigma_0 = \frac{4\kappa {M_{\rm pl \times \Big(-\frac{1 {2 c \phi_0^2 \sigma_0\Big) \ .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: As previously explained, $\dot\sigma_0$ is inversely proportional to $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-119: From Fig.~\ref{fig:S4 , we can see that, for a significant portion of the parameter space, $\Delta \ll 1$ when the phase transition begins to complete.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Consequently, we expect an enhanced contribution to the non-Gaussianity.
elements.sent-2411-121: As a result, for generic models of the spectator sector with power-law interactions between the inflaton field and the spectator field, as shown in Eq.~(\ref{eq:V ), we have \bea \dot\sigma_0 = \frac{\kappa \eta_5 m_S \dot \phi_0 {M_{\rm pl \Delta \ , \eea where $\eta_5$ encapsulates all the ${\cal O (1)$ effects.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-122: By substituting Eqs.~ (\ref{eq:eta1 ), (\ref{eq:eta2 ), (\ref{eq:eta3 ), and (\ref{eq:sigmadot ) into Eq.~(\ref{eq:3pt ), we obtain the three-point interaction of the curvature perturbation: \bea \lambda(\tau) {\cal R ^3 \ , \eea where \bea \lambda(\tau) = \frac{\kappa^3 m_S^4 \dot\phi_0^3 {M_{\rm pl ^3 H^3 \left[\frac{\eta_1\eta_5 {2\Delta \textcolor{black {+ \frac{\eta_2 \eta_5^2 {2\Delta^2 \textcolor{black {-}\frac{\eta_3 \eta_5^3 {6\Delta^3 \right] \ .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: It is important to note that the three-point interaction can still be generated through loop diagrams.
elements.sent-2411-124: However, these contributions are inherently small and thus can be reasonably neglected.
elements.sent-2411-125: We employ the standard method for calculating non-Gaussianity~\cite{Weinberg:2005vy,Weinberg:2006ac,Chen2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-126: Specifically, the three-point correlation function for $\mathcal{R}$ in our model corresponds to a contact diagram with a time-dependent coupling.
elements.sent-2411-127: Thus, the reduced three-point correlation function of $\mathcal{R}$ can be expressed as follows: \bea \langle {\cal R ({\bf k _1) {\cal R ({\bf k _2) {\cal R ({\bf k _3) \rangle' = \frac{3 {4}\int_{-\infty ^0 d\tau a^4(\tau) \lambda(\tau) \left[\frac{H^4 {\dot\phi_0^2}\right]^3 \frac{\tau^3 {k_1^2 k_2^2 k_3^2 f(k_1,k_2,k_3,\tau) \ , \eea where $\lambda(\tau)$ represents the three-point coupling of the curvature perturbation $\mathcal{R}$, as given in Eq.~(\ref{eq:R3 ).}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Note that the prime indicates that the delta function associated with momentum conservation has been omitted.
elements.sent-2411-129: The remaining terms in equation \eqref{eq:R3I originate from the inflaton bulk-to-boundary propagators.}
elements.sent-2411-130: For convenience in subsequent calculations, we define the function $f(k_1,k_2,k_3,\tau)$ as follows: \bea f(k_1,k_2,k_3,\tau) = {\rm Re \left[ \left(1+\frac{i {k_1\tau}\right) \left(1+\frac{i {k_2\tau}\right) \left(1+\frac{i {k_3\tau}\right) e^{i(k_1+k_2+k_3)\tau}\right].}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: For the time-dependent coupling $\lambda(\tau)$, when $\tau$ is far from $\tau_*$ (the conformal time at the phase transition), the speed of its evolution is proportional to the slow-roll parameters.
elements.sent-2411-132: However, as $\tau$ approaches $\tau_*$, and in the parameter regime where $\Delta \ll 1$ at $\tau_*$, substituting into equation \eqref{eq:R3 yields: \bea \frac{\partial\lambda {\partial\tau \sim \frac{\partial\lambda {\partial\Delta \frac{\partial\Delta {\partial\tau \sim \frac{1 {\Delta^4(\tau_*) \ .}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: This simplification allows us to focus on the enhancement induced by $\Delta$, which plays an important role in calculating $f_{\rm NL}$.
elements.sent-2411-134: Before the modes (${\bf k _1$, ${\bf k _2$, ${\bf k _3$ in (\ref{eq:R3I )) exit the horizon, the factor $f(k_1,k_2,k_3)$ oscillates rapidly with $\tau$, leading to a suppressed contribution to the $\tau$ integral.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-135: However, for modes that are completely outside the horizon, this factor can be approximated as a constant: \bea f(k_1,k_2,k_3) \rightarrow \frac{1 {3 \frac{k_1^3 + k_2^3 +k_3^3 {k_1 k_2 k_3 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: Considering the behavior of $\lambda(\tau)$ and $f(k_1, k_2, k_3)$ as discussed above, we can now highlight a crucial feature of the integral in Eq.~\eqref{eq:R3I .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Specifically, before $\tau$ reaches $\tau_*$, the $\tau$ integral takes the form $\int d\tau (1/\tau)$, indicating that each scale contributes equally to the integral until the phase transition is complete.
elements.sent-2411-138: To qualitatively estimate $f_{\rm NL}$, we focus on the region that $\Delta \ll 1$ in this section.
elements.sent-2411-139: We neglect the variations of $\kappa$ and $\dot\phi_0$ in the $\tau$ integral of Eq.~(\ref{eq:R3I ).}
elements.sent-2411-140: Furthermore, based on the specific interaction between the inflaton and spectator field in Eq.~\eqref{eq:V and the general parameterization for a first-order phase transition potential in Eq.~\eqref{eq:Vsigma , the parameters in the specific interaction can be re-expressed in terms of those in the general parameterization for convenience in subsequent calculations: \bea A v_1^2 v_2^2 = m_0^2 - c\phi_0^2 \ .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: Given that we are in the slow-roll regime, where the slow-roll parameter and the Hubble parameter can be well approximated as constants, the above differential equation can be solved using the initial conditions at the time of the phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-142: We should reiterate that quantities evaluated at the phase transition are denoted by the subscript $*$.

elements.sent-2411-186: By substituting $\lambda_1$, $\lambda_2$ and $\lambda_3$ into equation \eqref{eq:fnlgeneral and completing the $\tau$ integral within the region where $|k_{\rm max \tau| < 1$, we obtain the expression for the bispectrum: \begin{equation}B({\bf k_1},{\bf k_2},{\bf k_3}) \approx \fr{1}{4} \fr{k_1^3+k_2^3+k_3^3}{k_1^3k_2^3k_3^3} \Bigg[ \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^2 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^2 \int \fr{\d \tau }{(\epsilon_*)^{1/2} \tau} g_1(\Delta) h_1(\Delta_*) \nonumber + \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^3 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^3 \int \fr{\d \tau }{\epsilon_* \tau} g_2(\Delta) h_2(\Delta_*) \nonumber + \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^3 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^3 \int \fr{\d \tau }{\epsilon_* \tau} g_3(\Delta) h_3(\Delta_*) \Bigg] \label{eq:bispectrumgeneral}\end{equation} Specifically, only $\mathcal{G _1$ exhibits an additional dependence on the slow-roll parameter $\epsilon$, while the others only show dependence on $\Delta_*$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}B({\bf k_1},{\bf k_2},{\bf k_3}) \approx \fr{1}{4} \fr{k_1^3+k_2^3+k_3^3}{k_1^3k_2^3k_3^3} \Bigg[ \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^2 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^2 \int \fr{\d \tau }{(\epsilon_*)^{1/2} \tau} g_1(\Delta) h_1(\Delta_*) \nonumber + \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^3 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^3 \int \fr{\d \tau }{\epsilon_* \tau} g_2(\Delta) h_2(\Delta_*) \nonumber + \left(\fr{H^4}{\dot{\phi}_0^2}\right)^2 \left( \fr{\beta_*}{H} \right)^3 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right) \left( \fr{\Delta_*}{S_{E*}} \right)^3 \int \fr{\d \tau }{\epsilon_* \tau} g_3(\Delta) h_3(\Delta_*) \Bigg] \label{eq:bispectrumgeneral}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: This arises because the integral is taken over $\tau$, whereas the integrand is expressed in terms of $\Delta$.
elements.sent-2411-188: With this in mind, we can now explicitly present the expression for the amplitude of non-Gaussianity, $f_{\text{NL }$, as derived from equation \eqref{eq:bispectrumgeneral : \begin{equation}\label{eq:fnlgeneral1} \begin{split} f_{\text{NL}} =\Big(\frac{\beta_*}{H}\Big)^2\Big(\frac{L}{\rho_{\text{inf}}}\Big)\Big(\frac{\Delta_{*}}{\ma{S}_{E*}}\Big)^2\Big(\frac{M_{\rm pl}}{\phi}\Big)\ma{G}_1(\Delta_*) ~~~+\Big(\frac{\beta_*}{H}\Big)^3\Big(\frac{L}{\rho_{\text{inf}}}\Big)\Big(\frac{\Delta_{*}}{\ma{S}_{E*}}\Big)^3\ma{G}_2(\Delta_*) ~~~+\Big(\frac{\beta_*}{H}\Big)^3\Big(\frac{L}{\rho_{\text{inf}}}\Big)\Big(\frac{\Delta_{*}}{\ma{S}_{E*}}\Big)^3\ma{G}_3(\Delta_*), \end{split}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\ma{G}_1(\Delta_*) = \int \fr{\d \tau }{(\epsilon_*)^{1/2} \tau} g_1(\Delta) h_1(\Delta_*), \quad \ma{G}_{2,3}(\Delta_*) = \int \fr{\d \tau }{\epsilon_* \tau} g_{2,3}(\Delta) h_{2,3}(\Delta_*) \ ,\end{equation} where we introduce the functions $\mathcal{G _{1,2,3 (\Delta_*)$ to encapsulate the integral in Eq.~\eqref{eq:fnlgeneral , thereby simplifying our notation.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: We present the numerical result for $\mathcal{G _{1,2,3}$ in Fig. \ref{fig:fnlandF2 , which display a mild dependence on $\Delta_*$.}}

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{FigFNLvsGWPoly.pdf} \caption{The numerical results for the non-Gaussianity $f_\text{NL}$ as a function of the peak gravitational wave energy density $\Omega_\text{GW}^{\text{peak}}$ for the $\sigma^6$ model, evaluated over the range $\kappa_* \in (0.01, 1)$, with fixed parameters $\theta = 1/50$ and $\epsilon_* = 10^{-5}$. The yellow shaded region represents the sensitivity limits from Planck 2018~\cite{Planck:2019kim}, while the red and blue shaded regions denote the projected sensitivity of forthcoming Large-Scale Structure~\cite{SPHEREx:2014bgr} and 21 cm surveys~\cite{Munoz:2015eqa,Meerburg:2016zdz}. } \label{fig:fNLvsGW}

Shaded regions indicate the sensitivity limits, defined consistently with those in Fig.~\ref{fig:fNLvsGW}.} \label{fig:fNLvsGWCW}

elements.sent-2411-194: Both the primary and secondary GW signals induced by first-order phase transitions during inflation have been studied~\cite{An:2020fff,An:2022cce,An:2022toi,An:2023idh,An:2023jxf .}
elements.sent-2411-195: The signal strength of the GW signals depend on various powers of $H/\beta$ and $L/\rho_{\rm inf}$, as well as $\kappa$.
elements.sent-2411-196: Specifically, the spectrum of the first and secondary GW are given by \begin{equation}\Omega_GW^{(1)}(f) = \Omega_R \left( \fr{H}{\beta} \right)^5 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right)^2 \ma{F}_1 (\fr{f}{f_peak}) \Omega_GW^{(2)}(f) = \fr{\Omega_R}{\epsilon^2} \left( \fr{H}{\beta} \right)^6 \left( \fr{L}{\rho_inf} \right)^4 \ma{F}_2 (\fr{f}{f_peak})\end{equation} where $\Omega_\text{R}$ represents the radiation energy density of the universe, while $\epsilon$ denotes the slow-roll parameter following the phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-197: The functions $\ma{F _{1,2}$ correspond to the shape profiles of the primary and secondary gravitational waves, with their detailed derivation provided in ~\cite{An:2020fff,An:2022cce,An:2022toi,An:2023idh,An:2023jxf .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Notably, in comparison to the primary gravitational waves, the amplitude of the secondary gravitational waves generated during the phase transition is inherently amplified by the slow-roll parameter, potentially leading to a detectable signal in PTA, and therefore it will be the focus of our following discussions.
elements.sent-2411-199: Fig. \ref{fig:fnlandF2 illustrates the profile of $\ma{F _2$, which exhibits a global peak.}}
elements.sent-2411-200: This peak will be utilized as a defining characteristic of the gravitational wave signal’s strength in subsequent analyses.
elements.sent-2411-201: It is important to emphasize that the same set of parameters ($\epsilon$, $\beta$, and $\theta$) govern both the size of the curvature non-Gaussianity, $f_{\rm NL}$ and the GW spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-202: In Fig. \ref{fig:fNLvsGW , we present the numerical results of $f_\text{NL}$ vs the peak amplitude of the secondary gravitational wave spectrum, $\Omega_\text{GW ^{\text{peak }$ with different values of $\beta/H$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-203: In the figure, the constraint on $f_{\rm NL}$ from the Planck result~\cite{Planck:2019kim is also shown together with the future sensitivities of the Large Scale Structure Survey~\cite{SPHEREx:2014bgr and 21cm survey~\cite{Munoz:2015eqa,Meerburg:2016zdz .}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: We can see that for a large part of the parameter space, the $f_{\rm NL}$ corresponding to the phase transition during inflation can be observed by future experiments.
elements.sent-2411-205: In the previous sections, our analysis focuses on the case where the field $\sigma$ is governed by a polynomial potential, a form commonly arising in the context of effective field theory.
elements.sent-2411-206: In this section, we shift focus to a different class of potentials, specifically the CW potential, which typically emerges from quantum corrections at the loop level.
elements.sent-2411-207: The analysis of the primordial non-Gaussianity, $f_{\rm NL}$ and the GW signal are in parallel to the polynomial potential case.
elements.sent-2411-208: Therefore, we only list the results in this section.
elements.sent-2411-209: To be specific, we consider the following potential for the spectator field $\sigma$, \be V_\sigma^{(\text{CW ) = \fr{1 {2 \mu_\text{eff ^2 \sigma^2 - \fr{\lambda {4 \sigma^4 +\fr{\rho {4 \sigma^4 \log \left(\fr{\sigma^2 {\Lambda^2}\right).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-210: As in the previous discussion, the evolution of $\mu_\text{eff}$ will ultimately induce a first-order phase transition in the spectator sector, producing a distinctive gravitational wave signal.
elements.sent-2411-211: A detailed analysis of this gravitational wave signal can be found in~\cite{An:2022cce .}
elements.sent-2411-212: The calculation of $f_{\rm NL}$ can be done in parallel to the case of the polynomial potential as discussed in previous sections.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, due to the logarithmic term, there are no analytical expressions for $c$ and $\lambda$ unlike in the case of the polynomial potential.
elements.sent-2411-214: Therefore, we only present the numerical results here.
elements.sent-2411-215: In Fig.~\ref{fig:fNLvsGWCW , we present the plot of $|f_{\rm NL |$ vs $\Omega_{\rm GW ^{\rm peak}$ for $\beta/H = 10, 20, 30, 40$, $\kappa_* \in (0.01,1)$, $\theta = 1/50$, and $\epsilon_* = 10^{-5}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: We can observe that in the CW potential case, just like in the case of the polynomial potential, in a large part of the parameter space, observable $f_{\rm NL}$ can appear corresponding to the GW signal.
elements.sent-2411-217: In Fig.~\ref{fig:CWvssigma6 , we show the comparison of the results of $f_{\rm NL}$ and the GW signal between the polynomial model and the CW model.}
elements.sent-2411-218: We can see that for the same choices of the phenomenological parameters, such as $\beta/H$ and $\kappa$, $f_{\rm NL}$ in the two models only differ by an order one parameter.
elements.sent-2411-219: Therefore, we can believe that our conclusion that the phase transition in the spectator sector during inflation induces observable $f_{\rm NL}$ is robust against the choices of phase transition models.
elements.sent-2411-220: In this work, we explore the non-Gaussianity generated alongside a phase transition that occurs during inflation.
elements.sent-2411-221: We assume that the phase transition takes place within a spectator sector and is triggered by the evolution of the inflaton field.
elements.sent-2411-222: Our findings indicate that, in general, the magnitude of the non-Gaussianity, denoted as $f_{\rm NL}$, is of order one and could be detectable in future cosmological observations.
elements.sent-2411-223: We derive that $f_{\rm NL}$ is proportional to $(\beta/H)^3$, while the gravitational wave signal, $\Omega_{\rm GW}$ is proportional to $(\beta/H)^{-6}$.
elements.sent-2411-224: Consequently, the primordial non-Gaussianity serves as a complement to the gravitational wave signal when searching for phase transitions during inflation.
elements.sent-2411-225: Moreover, as we demonstrate in Sections~\ref{sec:NGvsGW and \ref{sec:CW , there exists a substantial region of parameter space where both gravitational wave signals and $f_{\rm NL}$ can be observed in the future.}}
elements.sent-2411-226: Furthermore, as discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:NG , since the $\tau$ integral in the calculation of $f_{\rm NL}$ sensitive to both the UV and IR cut-offs, the non-Gaussianity, $f_{\rm NL}$ produced in this way slightly depends on $k_{\rm max}$.}
elements.sent-2411-227: The typical value of index $n_{\rm NG = \partial\ln f_{\rm NL /\partial\ln k_{\rm max}$ is about ${\cal O (0.1)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: In this work, we focused on the scenario that a ${\cal Z _2$ symmetry restoration first-order phase transition triggered by the evolution of the inflaton field during inflation as an illustration of the calculation for the non-Gaussianity corresponding to the phase transition.}
elements.sent-2411-229: For symmetry-breaking phase transitions, in the symmetry-breaking phase, a 3pt interaction of the ${\cal R}$ will induced by the evolution of $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-230: Thus, in this case, the UV cutoff of the $\tau$ integral in Eq.~(\ref{eq:R3I ) is the time of the phase transition $\tau_\star$, instead of $k_{\rm max ^{-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-231: The IR cutoff can be set at the time when inflation ends.
elements.sent-2411-232: The form of the integrand is similar to the symmetry-restoration case.
elements.sent-2411-233: Thus, we expect that $f_{\rm NL}$ contributed by the spectator sector in the symmetry breaking case has the similar size of that from the symmetry restoration case as discussed in detail in this work.
elements.sent-2411-234: However, $f_{NL}$ produced in the symmetry breaking case will have no scale dependence.
elements.sent-2411-235: For second-order phase transitions, we also expect to have an ${\cal O}$(1) $f_{\rm NL}$.
elements.sent-2411-236: However, as we saw in the discussion in Sec.~\ref{sec:NG , the $\tau$ integral around the phase transition point significantly contributes to $f_{\rm NL}$.}
elements.sent-2411-237: The behavior of the spectator field at the phase transition point differs, necessitating a distinct approach for second-order phase transitions.
elements.sent-2411-238: We leave the discussion for second-order phase transition in future work.
